volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.2.152)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Salutem primum iam a principio propitiam
sections.translation: In the first [*] place now , Spectators , at the commencement , do I wish health and happiness [*] to myself and to you . I bring you Plautus , with my tongue , not with my hand : I beg that you will receive him with favouring ears .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: nunc argumentum accipite atque animum advortite ;
sections.translation: Now learn the argument , and give your attention ; in as few words as possible will I be brief .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.7
sections.original: atque hoc poetae faciunt in comoediis :
sections.translation: But in their Comedies the poets do this ; they feign that all the business takes place at Athens [*] , in order that it may appear the more Grecian to you .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: ego nusquam dicam nisi ubi factum dicitur .
sections.translation: I will not tell you that this matter happened anywhere except where it is said to have happened .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.11
sections.original: atque adeo hoc argumentum graecissat , tamen
sections.translation: And , in fact , this subject is a Greek one ; still , it is not an Attic [*] , but a Sicilian one .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.13
sections.original: huic argumento antelogium hoc fuit ;
sections.translation: This has been my preface to the subject of this play . Now will I give the subject , meted out to you ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: non modio , neque trimodio , verum ipso horreo :
sections.translation: not in a measure , nor yet in a threefold measure [*] , but in the granary itself ; so great is my heartiness in telling you the plot .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.17
sections.original: mercator quidam fuit Syracusis senex ,
sections.translation: There was a certain aged man , a merchant at Syracuse [*] ; to him two sons were born , twins , children so like in appearance that their own foster-mother [*] ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: non internosse posset quae mammam dabat ,
sections.translation: who gave the breast , was not able to distinguish them , nor even the mother herself who had given them birth ; as a person , indeed , informed me who had seen the children ; I never saw them , let no one of you fancy so . After the children were now seven years old , the father

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: oneravit navem magnam multis mercibus ;
sections.translation: freighted a large ship with much merchandize . The father put one of the twins on board the ship , and took him away , together with himself , to traffic at Tarentum [*] ; the other one he left with his mother at home . By accident , there were games at Tarentum when he came there :

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: mortales multi , ut ad ludos , convenerant :
sections.translation: many persons , as generally happens at the games , had met together ; the child strayed away there from his father among the people . A certain merchant of Epidamnus was there ; he picked up the child , and carried it away to Epidamnus [*] . But its father , after he had lost the child ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: animum despondit , eaque is aegritudine
sections.translation: took it heavily to heart , and through grief at it he died a few days after at Tarentum . Now , after news reached the grandfather of the children at home about this matter , how that one of the children had been stolen ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: immutat nomen avos huic gemino alteri ;
sections.translation: the grandfather changed the name of that other twin . So much did he love that one which had been stolen , that he gave his name to the one that was at home . That you may not mistake hereafter , I tell you then this beforehand ; the name of both the twin-brothers is the same . He gave the same name of Menaechmus to this one as the other had ; and by the same name the grandfather himself was called .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.45
sections.original: ( propterea illius nomen memini facilius ,
sections.translation: I remember his name the more easily for the reason that I saw him cried with much noise [*] .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.49
sections.original: nunc in Epidamnum pedibus redeundum est mihi ,
sections.translation: Now must I speed back on foot to Epidamnus ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: ut hanc rem vobis examussim disputem .
sections.translation: that I may exactly disclose this matter to you . If any one of you [*] wishes anything to be transacted for him at Epidamnus , command me boldly and speak out ; but on these terms , that he give me the means by which it may be transacted for him . For unless a person gives the money , he will be mistaken ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: qui dederit , magis maiores nugas egerit .
sections.translation: ( in a lower tone ) except that he who does give it will be very much more mistaken [*] . But I have returned to that place whence I set forth , and yet I am standing in the self-same spot . This person of Epidamnus , whom I mentioned just now , that stole that other twin child , had no children , except his wealth .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: adoptat illum puerum surrupticium
sections.translation: He adopted as his son the child so carried off , and gave him a well-portioned wife , and made him his heir when he himself died . For as , by chance , he was going into the country , when it had rained heavily , entering , not far from the city , a rapid stream ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.65
sections.original: rapidus raptori pueri subduxit pedes
sections.translation: in its rapidity [*] it threw the ravisher of the child off his legs ; and hurried the man away to great and grievous destruction . And so a very large fortune fell to that youth . Here ( pointing to the house ) does the stolen twin now dwell . Now that twin , who dwells at Syracuse ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.70
sections.original: hodie in Epidamnum veniet cum servo suo
sections.translation: has come this day to Epidamnus with his servant to make enquiry for this own twin-brother of his . This is the city of Epidamnus while this play is acting ; when another shall be acted , it will become another town ; just as our companies , too , are wont to be shifted about .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.75
sections.original: modo † ni caditat leno , modo adulescens , modo senex ,
sections.translation: The same person now acts the procurer , now the youth , now the old man , the pauper , the beggar , the king , the parasite , the soothsayer ⋯

sections.label: Section 1.1.77
sections.original: Iuventus nomen fecit Peniculo mihi ,
sections.translation: The young men have given me the name of Peniculus [*] , for this reason , because when I eat , I wipe the tables clean . ⋯ The persons who bind captives with chains ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: et qui fugitivis servis indunt compedes ,
sections.translation: and who put fetters upon runaway slaves , act very foolishly , in my opinion at least . For if bad usage is added to his misfortune for a wretched man , the greater is his inclination to run away and to do amiss . For by some means or other do they release themselves from the chains ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: tum compediti ánum lima praeterunt
sections.translation: while thus fettered , they either wear away a link with a file , or else with a stone they knock out the nail ; ’ tis a mere trifle this . He whom you wish to keep securely that he may not run away , with meat and with drink ought he to be chained ; do you bind down the mouth of a man to a full table .

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: dum tu illi quod edit et quod potet praebeas ,
sections.translation: So long as you give him what to eat and what to drink at his own pleasure in abundance every day , i ’ faith he ’ ll never run away , even if he has committed an offence that ’ s capital ; easily will you secure him so long as you shall bind him with such chains . So very supple are these chains of food ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: quam magis extendas , tanto adstringunt artius .
sections.translation: the more you stretch them so much the more tightly do they bind . But now I ’ m going directly to Menaechmus ; whither for this long time I have been sentenced , thither of my own accord I am going , that he may enchain me . For , by my troth , this man does not nourish persons , but he quite rears and reinvigorates them ; no one administers medicine more agreably .

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: ita est adulescens ; ipsus escae maxumae
sections.translation: Such is this young man ; himself with a very well-stocked larder , he gives dinners fit for Ceres [*] ; so does he heap the tables up , and piles so vast of dishes does he arrange , you must stand on your couch if you wish for anything at the top . But I have now had an interval these many days ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: domi domítus sum usque cum caris meis .
sections.translation: while I ’ ve been lording it at home all along together with my dear ones [*] ;— for nothing do I eat or purchase but what it is most dear . But inasmuch as dear ones , when they are provided , are in the habit of forsaking us , I am now paying him a visit . But his door is opening ; and see , I perceive Menaechmus himself ; he is coming out of doors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: Ní mala , ni stúlta sies , ni índomita impósque animi ,
sections.translation: Unless you were worthless , unless you were foolish , unless you were stark wild and an idiot , that which you see is disagreable to your husband , you would deem to be so to yourself as well . Moreover , if after this day you do any such thing to me , I ’ ll force you , a divorced woman , turned out of my doors to go visit your father . For as often as I wish to go out of the house , you are detaining me , calling me back , asking me questions ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: quó ego eam , quám rem agam , quíd negotí geram ,
sections.translation: whither I am going , what matter I am about , what business I am transacting , what I am wanting , what I am bringing , what I have been doing out of doors ? I ’ ve surely brought home a custom-house officer [*] as my wife ; so much am I obliged to disclose all my business , whatever I have done and am doing . I ’ ve had you hitherto indulged too much . Now , therefore , I ’ ll tell you how I am about to act .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: quando égo tibi ancillás , penum ,
sections.translation: Since I find you handsomely in maids , provisions , wool , gold trinkets , garments , and purple , and you are wanting in nought , you ’ ll beware of a mischief if you ’ re wise ; you ’ ll leave off watching your husband . ( In a lower voice .) And therefore , that you mayn ’ t be watching me in vain , for your pains I shall find me a mistress to-day , and invite her to dinner somewhere out of doors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Illic homo se uxori simulat male loqui , loquitur mihi ;
sections.translation: This fellow pretends that he ’ s upbraiding his wife , but he ’ s addressing myself ; for if he does dine out of doors , he really is punishing me , not his wife .

sections.label: Section 1.2.127
sections.original: Euax , iurgio hercle tandem uxorem abegi ab ianua .
sections.translation: Hurra ! I ’ troth , by my taunts I ’ ve driven my wife from the door at last . Where now are your intriguing husbands ? Why do they hesitate , all returning thanks , to bring presents to me who have fought so gallantly ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: hánc modo uxori intus pallam surrupui , ad scortum fero .
sections.translation: This mantle [*] of my wife ’ s ( taking it from under his cloak ) I ’ ve just now stolen from in-doors , and I ’ m taking it to my mistress . This way it ’ s proper for a clever trick to be played this knowing husband-watcher . This is a becoming action , this is right , this is skilful , this is done in workman-like style ; inasmuch as at my own risk I ’ ve taken this from my plague , this same shall be carried off to destruction [*] . With the safety of my allies [*] I ’ ve gained a booty from the foe .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: Heús adulescens , ecqua in istac pars inest praeda mihi ?
sections.translation: Harkye ! young man ; pray what share have I [*] in that booty ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: Perii , in insidias deveni .
sections.translation: I ’ m undone ; I ’ ve fallen into an ambuscade .

sections.label: Section 1.2.136b
sections.original: Immo in praesidium , ne time .
sections.translation: Say a safeguard rather . Don ’ t be afraid .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137
sections.original: Quis homo est ?
sections.translation: What person ’ s this ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.137b
sections.original: Ego sum .
sections.translation: ’ Tis I .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137c
sections.original: O mea Commoditas , o mea Opportunitas ,
sections.translation: O my convenient friend — O my ready occasion , save you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138b
sections.original: Salve .
sections.translation: And save you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138c
sections.original: Quid agis ?
sections.translation: What are you about ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.138d
sections.original: Teneo dextera genium meum .
sections.translation: Holding my good Genius in my right hand .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Non potuisti magis per tempus mi advenire quam advenis .
sections.translation: You couldn ’ t have come to me more à propos than you have come .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: Ita ego soleo : commoditatis omnis articulos scio .
sections.translation: I ’ m in the habit of doing so ; I understand all the points of ready occasion .

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: Vin tu facinus luculentum inspicere ?
sections.translation: Would you like to be witness of a brilliant exploit ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.141b
sections.original: Quis id coxit coquos ?
sections.translation: What cook has cooked it ? I shall know at once if he has made any mistake , when I see the remnants . [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Dic mi , enumquam tu vidisti tabulam pictam in pariete ,
sections.translation: Tell me — did you ever see a picture painted on a wall , where the eagle is carrying off Ganymede [*] , or Venus Adonis ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Saepe . sed quid istaé picturae ad me attinent ?
sections.translation: Many a time . But what are these pictures to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.145b
sections.original: Age me aspice .
sections.translation: Come , look at me [*] . Do I at all bear any resemblance to them ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.146b
sections.original: Quis istest ornatus tuos ?
sections.translation: What ’ s this garb of yours ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.147
sections.original: Dic hominem lepidissimum esse me .
sections.translation: Pronounce me to be a very clever fellow .

sections.label: Section 1.2.147b
sections.original: Vbi essuri sumus ?
sections.translation: Where are we to feed ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.148
sections.original: Dic modo hoc quod ego te iubeo .
sections.translation: Only do you say that which I requested you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.148b
sections.original: Dico : homo lepidissime .
sections.translation: Well , I do say so ; very clever fellow .

sections.label: Section 1.2.149
sections.original: Ecquid audes de tuo istuc addere ?
sections.translation: And don ’ t you venture to add anything of your own to it ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.149b
sections.original: Atque hilarissime .
sections.translation: — And very pleasant fellow .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: Perge porro .
sections.translation: Go on .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150b
sections.original: Non pergo hercle , nisi scio qua gratia .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I really can ’ t go on , unless I know for what reason . You ’ ve had a fall-out with your wife ; on that ground am I the more strongly on my guard [*] against you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.152
sections.original: Clam uxoremst ubi pulchre habeamus atque hunc comburamus diem .
sections.translation: ⋯ where , unknown to my wife , we will erect the funeral pile ⋯ and let us consume this day [*] upon it .
